docs.title: Rinsed Snowflake Data Client
docs.text:
Python client for WashU Carwash Rinsed CRM data in Snowflake.

Provides fast, bulk access to historical KPI data via Snowflake — complementing the Sonny's Data API Client which is best suited for real-time data pulls.

docs.title: Why This Client?
docs.text: The Sonny's Data API has strict rate limits (20 requests per 15 seconds). For bulk historical analysis — monthly reports, trend analysis, churn tracking — Snowflake is significantly faster. This client mirrors the Sonny's client patterns (client.stats.*) so the API feels familiar.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient

with RinsedClient() as client:
    # Car counts
    cars = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    print(f"Total washes: {cars.total}")

    # Revenue
    rev = client.stats.retail_revenue("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    print(f"Retail revenue: ${rev.total:,.2f}")

    # Conversion rate
    conv = client.stats.conversion_rate("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    print(f"Conversion: {conv.rate:.1%}")

    # All KPIs at once
    report = client.stats.report("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install git+https://github.com/christopher-nance/Rinsed-Snowflake-Data-Client.git

Or from source for development:

git clone https://github.com/christopher-nance/Rinsed-Snowflake-Data-Client.git
cd Rinsed-Snowflake-Data-Client
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Available KPIs
docs.text:
All methods are on client.stats and accept start, end, and optional locations:

Method Returns Description total_car_count() CarCountResult Total washes (retail + member + free) retail_car_count() CarCountResult Retail (non-member) wash count member_car_count() CarCountResult Membership redemption count retail_revenue() RevenueResult Revenue from retail washes membership_revenue() MembershipRevenueResult Revenue from memberships (new + renewed) average_wash_price() AWPResult Retail ticket average new_membership_sales() MembershipSalesResult New + rejoin membership count conversion_rate() ConversionResult Sales / eligible washes voluntary_churn_rate() ChurnResult Member-initiated cancellations involuntary_churn_rate() ChurnResult Expired / failed payment churn report() StatsReport All KPIs bundled

docs.title: rinsed_snowflake_client.RinsedClient(account: str | None = None, user: str | None = None, password: str | None = None, warehouse: str | None = None, database: str | None = None, schema: str | None = None, role: str | None = None)
docs.text:
Client for WashU Rinsed Snowflake data.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default account str | None

Snowflake account identifier. If None, loads from env.

None user str | None

Snowflake username.

None password str | None

Snowflake password.

None warehouse str | None

Snowflake warehouse name.

None database str | None

Snowflake database name.

None schema str | None

Snowflake schema name.

None role str | None

Optional Snowflake role.

None

Examples:

docs.title: From environment variables
docs.text: with RinsedClient() as client: result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31") print(result.total)

docs.title: Explicit credentials
docs.text: client = RinsedClient(account="...", user="...", password="...", warehouse="...", database="...", schema="...") client.connect() df = client.query("SELECT * FROM conversion_daily LIMIT 10") client.close()

docs.title: stats: StatsResource cached property
docs.text: Analytics and KPI methods.

docs.title: close() -> None
docs.text: Close the Snowflake connection.

docs.title: connect() -> RinsedClient
docs.text: Establish Snowflake connection.

docs.title: query(sql: str, params: Sequence | None = None) -> pd.DataFrame
docs.text:
Execute raw SQL and return a pandas DataFrame.

Power-user escape hatch for ad-hoc queries.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default sql str

SQL query string.

required params Sequence | None

Optional parameters for parameterized queries.

None

Returns:

Type Description DataFrame

Query results as a pandas DataFrame.

docs.title: rinsed_snowflake_client.resources.StatsResource(client: RinsedClient)
docs.text:
Analytics and KPI methods.

Access via client.stats.

docs.title: average_wash_price(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> AWPResult
docs.text: Average Wash Price = retail revenue / retail car count.

docs.title: conversion_rate(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> ConversionResult
docs.text: Conversion rate = sales / eligible washes (from CONVERSION_DAILY).

docs.title: involuntary_churn_rate(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> ChurnResult
docs.text: Involuntary churn rate (expired memberships / failed payments).

docs.title: member_car_count(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> CarCountResult
docs.text: Membership redemption (member wash) count.

docs.title: membership_revenue(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> MembershipRevenueResult
docs.text: Revenue from new + renewed memberships.

docs.title: new_membership_sales(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> MembershipSalesResult
docs.text: Count of new + rejoin membership sales.

docs.title: report(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> StatsReport
docs.text: All KPIs in a single call.

docs.title: retail_car_count(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> CarCountResult
docs.text: Retail (non-member) wash count.

docs.title: retail_revenue(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> RevenueResult
docs.text: Revenue from retail (non-member) washes.

docs.title: total_car_count(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> CarCountResult
docs.text: Total washes (retail + member + free) from CONVERSION_DAILY.

docs.title: voluntary_churn_rate(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> ChurnResult
docs.text: Voluntary churn rate (member-initiated cancellations).

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text:
All exceptions inherit from RinsedError, allowing you to catch all client errors with a single handler:

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient, RinsedError

try:
    with RinsedClient() as client:
        result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
except RinsedError as e:
    print(f"Client error: {e}")

docs.title: Exception Hierarchy
docs.text:
RinsedError
├── ConfigurationError
├── ConnectionError
├── QueryError
└── ValidationError

docs.title: WashU vs. Rinsed Churn Definition
docs.text:
This client uses WashU's billing-cycle churn definition, which intentionally differs from Rinsed's real-time recording:

Rinsed WashU (this client) When churn is recorded The day the cancellation or expiry happens The month when the would-be recharge doesn't process Example Member billed 2/12, cancels 2/23 → February churn Same member → March churn (3/12 recharge never happens) Rationale Reflects real-time system state Member paid for February, so they're a February member

The key insight: WashU's churn for month M is equivalent to Rinsed's churn for month M-1, shifted forward by one billing cycle.

WashU Churn Month Equals Rinsed Churn Month February 2026 January 2026 March 2026 February 2026 April 2026 March 2026

Info

If you're comparing this client's churn output to Rinsed's dashboard, expect a one-month offset. The totals over a multi-month window will be the same — just shifted.

docs.title: Voluntary Churn
docs.text:
Member-initiated cancellations. The member chose to cancel their membership. In Rinsed's database, these are recorded as churn_type = 'terminated'.

result = client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

print(f"Voluntary churn rate: {result.rate:.2%}")    # 5.68%
print(f"Members who churned: {result.churned_count:,}")  # 2,271
print(f"Starting member base: {result.starting_count:,}")  # 39,976

docs.title: Involuntary Churn
docs.text:
Expired memberships due to failed payments — the member didn't actively cancel, but their payment method failed. In Rinsed's database, these are recorded as churn_type = 'expired'.

result = client.stats.involuntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

print(f"Involuntary churn rate: {result.rate:.2%}")  # 1.59%
print(f"Members who churned: {result.churned_count:,}")  # 637
print(f"Starting member base: {result.starting_count:,}")  # 39,976

docs.title: Combined Churn
docs.text:
To get total churn, call both methods and combine:

vol = client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
inv = client.stats.involuntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

total_churned = vol.churned_count + inv.churned_count
total_rate = total_churned / vol.starting_count  # same denominator
print(f"Total churn: {total_rate:.2%} ({total_churned:,} members)")

docs.title: Per-Location Breakdown
docs.text:
Both churn methods include a by_location list with per-location churn rates:

result = client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

# Rank locations by churn rate (highest first)
ranked = sorted(result.by_location, key=lambda x: x.value, reverse=True)
for loc in ranked:
    print(f"{loc.location_name}: {loc.value:.2%}")
# Naperville: 14.74%
# Berwyn: 11.89%
# Evergreen Park: 11.03%
# ...

Note

The value field in by_location is the churn rate for that location (not the count). Each location has its own denominator based on its active member count.

docs.title: Numerator (Churned Members)
docs.text:
From MEMBER_HISTORY: count of distinct members whose created_month (last billing month) was the month before the requested period and who have a churn_type set.

Why the previous month? Under WashU's definition, a member billed in January who cancels in January is still a January member. Their churn is recognized in February — when their would-be recharge doesn't happen.

docs.title: Denominator (Starting Member Base)
docs.text: From ACTIVE_MEMBERS_MONTHLY (Rinsed definition): total active members for the month before the requested period. This represents the member base that was "at risk" of churning.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
churn_rate = churned_count / starting_count

Where:

  • churned_count = members last billed in month M-1 with churn_type set
  • starting_count = active members in month M-1

docs.title: Monthly Churn Trend
docs.text:
from datetime import datetime

with RinsedClient() as client:
    months = [
        ("2025-10-01", "2025-10-31"),
        ("2025-11-01", "2025-11-30"),
        ("2025-12-01", "2025-12-31"),
        ("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31"),
        ("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28"),
    ]

    print(f"{'Month':<12} {'Voluntary':>10} {'Involuntary':>12} {'Total':>8}")
    print("-" * 44)

    for start, end in months:
        vol = client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate(start, end)
        inv = client.stats.involuntary_churn_rate(start, end)
        total = (vol.churned_count + inv.churned_count) / vol.starting_count
        label = datetime.fromisoformat(start).strftime("%b %Y")
        print(f"{label:<12} {vol.rate:>9.2%} {inv.rate:>11.2%} {total:>7.2%}")

docs.title: Location-Level Churn Report
docs.text:
with RinsedClient() as client:
    vol = client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
    inv = client.stats.involuntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

    vol_map = {l.location_name: l.value for l in vol.by_location}
    inv_map = {l.location_name: l.value for l in inv.by_location}

    print(f"{'Location':<16} {'Voluntary':>10} {'Involuntary':>12} {'Total':>8}")
    print("-" * 48)
    for loc in sorted(vol_map):
        v = vol_map[loc]
        i = inv_map.get(loc, 0)
        print(f"{loc:<16} {v:>9.2%} {i:>11.2%} {v+i:>7.2%}")

docs.title: Retention Rate
docs.text:
Retention is the inverse of churn:

vol = client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
inv = client.stats.involuntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

total_churn = (vol.churned_count + inv.churned_count) / vol.starting_count
retention = 1 - total_churn
print(f"Retention rate: {retention:.2%}")

docs.title: Annualized Churn
docs.text:
result = client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
annual = 1 - (1 - result.rate) ** 12
print(f"Annualized voluntary churn: {annual:.1%}")

docs.title: Month Boundary
docs.text:
Churn methods operate on monthly boundaries. The start date determines which month is used:

# Both of these query the same churn data (February churn = members last billed in January)
client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate("2026-02-15", "2026-02-28")  # same result

The start date's month is what matters. The end date is not used for churn calculations — it's included in the result metadata for consistency.

docs.title: New Locations
docs.text: Locations that didn't exist in the previous month will have zero starting members and won't appear in churn results. They'll start appearing once they have a full month of active member data.

docs.title: Wheaton
docs.text: Wheaton is not present in all churn validation data due to its later onboarding. The client will return data for Wheaton when available in the underlying tables.

docs.title: Small Locations
docs.text: Locations with very few members (e.g., Jackson with ~290) can show volatile churn rates. A single cancellation has an outsized impact on the percentage. Consider this when comparing locations of different sizes.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: All exceptions inherit from RinsedError, so you can catch everything with a single handler or handle specific error types individually.

docs.title: Exception Hierarchy
docs.text:
RinsedError                    # Base — catch-all for any client error
├── ConfigurationError         # Missing/invalid Snowflake credentials
├── ConnectionError            # Can't connect to Snowflake
├── QueryError                 # SQL execution failed
└── ValidationError            # Bad input (dates, locations)

docs.title: Catching All Errors
docs.text:
from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient, RinsedError

try:
    with RinsedClient() as client:
        result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
except RinsedError as e:
    print(f"Something went wrong: {e}")

docs.title: ConfigurationError
docs.text:
Raised when Snowflake credentials are missing or incomplete. This happens at client creation — before any queries are made.

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient, ConfigurationError

try:
    client = RinsedClient()  # fails if env vars missing
except ConfigurationError as e:
    print(e)
    # "Missing required environment variables: SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT, SNOWFLAKE_PASSWORD. ..."

When using explicit credentials, all six required fields must be provided:

try:
    client = RinsedClient(account="acct", user="usr")  # missing password, warehouse, etc.
except ConfigurationError as e:
    print(e)
    # "When providing explicit credentials, account, user, password, warehouse, database, and schema are all required."

Tip

Check your .env file if you get this error. Common causes:

  • .env file exists but load_dotenv() wasn't called before creating the client
  • Environment variable name is misspelled (e.g., SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT vs SNOWFLAKE_ACCT)
  • .env file is in a different directory than your script

docs.title: ConnectionError
docs.text:
Raised when the client can't establish a Snowflake connection. Common causes: wrong credentials, network issues, account not found.

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient, ConnectionError

try:
    with RinsedClient() as client:
        result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
except ConnectionError as e:
    print(f"Can't connect: {e}")

Also raised when you try to query without connecting first:

client = RinsedClient()
# forgot client.connect()

try:
    client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
except ConnectionError as e:
    print(e)  # "Not connected. Use connect() or a context manager."

Snowflake account format

The SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT value must include the region identifier if applicable (e.g., xy12345.us-east-1). An incorrect account string will result in a ConnectionError.

docs.title: QueryError
docs.text:
Raised when SQL execution fails. The error message includes the first 100 characters of the SQL for debugging.

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient, QueryError

with RinsedClient() as client:
    try:
        df = client.query("SELECT * FROM nonexistent_table")
    except QueryError as e:
        print(f"Bad query: {e}")

Common causes:

  • Table or column doesn't exist
  • SQL syntax error
  • Permission denied on a table
  • Snowflake session timeout on very long queries

docs.title: ValidationError
docs.text:
Raised when input parameters fail validation — before any Snowflake query is made.

Bad date format:

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient, ValidationError

with RinsedClient() as client:
    try:
        client.stats.total_car_count("not-a-date", "2026-01-31")
    except ValidationError as e:
        print(e)  # "Invalid date format: 'not-a-date'. Expected ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD) or datetime object."

Bad location type:

    try:
        client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31", locations=123)
    except ValidationError as e:
        print(e)  # "Invalid date type: expected string or datetime, got int."

Empty location string:

    try:
        client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31", locations="")
    except ValidationError as e:
        print(e)  # "Location cannot be empty string."

docs.title: Recommended Pattern
docs.text:
For production scripts, catch specific errors to provide meaningful feedback:

from rinsed_snowflake_client import (
    RinsedClient,
    ConfigurationError,
    ConnectionError,
    QueryError,
    RinsedError,
)

try:
    with RinsedClient() as client:
        report = client.stats.report("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
        print(f"Total cars: {report.total_car_count.total:,}")

except ConfigurationError:
    print("Check your .env file — Snowflake credentials are missing.")

except ConnectionError:
    print("Can't reach Snowflake. Check network and credentials.")

except QueryError as e:
    print(f"A query failed: {e}")

except RinsedError as e:
    print(f"Unexpected client error: {e}")

docs.title: Logging & Debugging
docs.text:
For debugging Snowflake connection issues, enable the Snowflake connector's built-in logging:

import logging
logging.basicConfig()
logging.getLogger("snowflake.connector").setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

with RinsedClient() as client:
    result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")

This will show detailed connection handshake, query execution, and network timing information.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install git+https://github.com/christopher-nance/Rinsed-Snowflake-Data-Client.git

Or install from source for development:

git clone https://github.com/christopher-nance/Rinsed-Snowflake-Data-Client.git
cd Rinsed-Snowflake-Data-Client
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: The client needs Snowflake credentials. You can provide them via environment variables or pass them directly.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Create a .env file (see .env.example in the repo):

SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT=your_account_identifier
SNOWFLAKE_USER=your_username
SNOWFLAKE_PASSWORD=your_password
SNOWFLAKE_WAREHOUSE=your_warehouse
SNOWFLAKE_DATABASE=your_database
SNOWFLAKE_SCHEMA=your_schema
SNOWFLAKE_ROLE=your_role

Load them with python-dotenv before creating the client:

from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient

with RinsedClient() as client:
    result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    print(result.total)

Warning

Never commit your .env file to version control. The repo's .gitignore already excludes it.

docs.title: Explicit Credentials
docs.text:
Pass credentials directly when you need to connect to multiple accounts or avoid environment variables:

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient

client = RinsedClient(
    account="your_account",
    user="your_user",
    password="your_password",
    warehouse="your_warehouse",
    database="your_database",
    schema="your_schema",
    role="your_role",  # optional
)
client.connect()
result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
client.close()

Note

When using explicit credentials, account, user, password, warehouse, database, and schema are all required. Passing only some of them raises ConfigurationError.

docs.title: Missing Configuration
docs.text:
If required environment variables are missing, ConfigurationError is raised immediately at client creation — not at query time:

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient, ConfigurationError

try:
    client = RinsedClient()  # fails if env vars are missing
except ConfigurationError as e:
    print(e)
    # "Missing required environment variables: SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT, SNOWFLAKE_PASSWORD. ..."

docs.title: Context Manager (Recommended)
docs.text:
The context manager automatically connects on entry and closes on exit, even if an exception occurs:

with RinsedClient() as client:
    cars = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    rev = client.stats.retail_revenue("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    # connection is automatically closed when the block exits

This is the recommended pattern for most use cases.

docs.title: Manual Connection
docs.text:
For long-running processes or interactive sessions:

client = RinsedClient()
client.connect()

# ... use client across multiple operations ...

client.close()  # always close when done

Warning

If you use manual connection management, always call client.close() in a finally block to avoid leaving Snowflake connections open:

client = RinsedClient()
client.connect()
try:
    result = client.stats.report("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
finally:
    client.close()

docs.title: Calling Without a Connection
docs.text:
If you call a method before connecting, you get a ConnectionError:

client = RinsedClient()
# forgot to call client.connect()
client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
# raises ConnectionError: "Not connected. Use connect() or a context manager."

docs.title: Date Parameters
docs.text:
All KPI methods require start and end dates. Both accept:

  • ISO format strings: "2026-01-01", "2026-12-31"
  • datetime objects: datetime(2026, 1, 1)
  • Mixed: one string, one datetime
from datetime import datetime

# All equivalent
client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
client.stats.total_car_count(datetime(2026, 1, 1), datetime(2026, 1, 31))
client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", datetime(2026, 1, 31))

docs.title: Single-Day Queries
docs.text:
Pass the same date for both start and end:

# Yesterday's total washes
result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-03-18", "2026-03-18")

docs.title: Invalid Dates
docs.text:
Bad date strings raise ValidationError:

client.stats.total_car_count("not-a-date", "2026-01-31")
# raises ValidationError: "Invalid date format: 'not-a-date'. ..."

docs.title: Date Ranges
docs.text:
There is no maximum date range. You can query years of data at once:

# Full year
result = client.stats.total_car_count("2025-01-01", "2025-12-31")

# Multi-year
result = client.stats.total_car_count("2024-01-01", "2026-03-18")

Tip

Unlike the Sonny's Data API Client (which has a 31-day max per request), this client has no date range limits. Snowflake handles large ranges efficiently.

docs.title: Location Filtering
docs.text:
All KPI methods accept an optional locations parameter:

# All locations (default) — Hub Office and Query Server are always excluded
client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")

# Single location
client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31", locations="Burbank")

# Multiple locations
client.stats.total_car_count(
    "2026-01-01", "2026-01-31",
    locations=["Burbank", "Carol Stream", "Des Plaines"],
)

docs.title: Location Names
docs.text:
Use the exact location names as they appear in Rinsed:

Location Berwyn Burbank Carol Stream Des Plaines Dickson Evergreen Park Fairview Jackson Joliet Naperville Niles Nolensville Plainfield Villa Park Wheaton

Note

Location names are case-sensitive. "burbank" will return no results — use "Burbank".

docs.title: Excluded Locations
docs.text: Hub Office and Query Server are always excluded from all queries, regardless of the locations parameter. These are internal/test locations that don't represent real wash sites.

docs.title: Invalid Locations
docs.text:
# Empty string raises ValidationError
client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31", locations="")

# Non-string in list raises ValidationError
client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31", locations=[123])

A valid location name that doesn't exist in the database will simply return zero results (no error).

docs.title: Result Objects
docs.text:
All KPI methods return typed Pydantic models. Most include:

  • A scalar aggregate (e.g., total, rate)
  • A by_location list with per-location breakdowns
  • period_start and period_end metadata
result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

# Aggregate
print(result.total)          # 288389
print(result.period_start)   # "2026-02-01"
print(result.period_end)     # "2026-02-28"

# Per-location breakdown
for loc in result.by_location:
    print(f"{loc.location_name}: {loc.value:,.0f}")
    # Burbank: 37,476
    # Carol Stream: 25,387
    # ...

docs.title: Serialization
docs.text:
Since all results are Pydantic models, you can easily convert them to dicts or JSON:

result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

# To dict
data = result.model_dump()

# To JSON string
json_str = result.model_dump_json(indent=2)

docs.title: Empty Results
docs.text:
If a query returns no data (e.g., a future date or a location with no activity), results are zero/empty rather than raising an error:

result = client.stats.total_car_count("2099-01-01", "2099-01-31")
print(result.total)          # 0
print(result.by_location)    # []

docs.title: Raw Queries
docs.text: For ad-hoc analysis beyond the built-in KPI methods, use client.query() to execute SQL against Snowflake. This returns a pandas DataFrame — useful for data exploration, custom aggregations, and one-off analyses.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient

with RinsedClient() as client:
    df = client.query("SELECT * FROM conversion_daily LIMIT 10")
    print(df)

docs.title: Parameterized Queries
docs.text:
Use %s placeholders for safe parameterized queries. This prevents SQL injection and handles type conversion automatically:

df = client.query(
    "SELECT * FROM fct_revenue WHERE location_name = %s AND DATE(created_at) = %s",
    ["Burbank", "2026-03-01"],
)

Multiple parameters:

df = client.query(
    """
    SELECT location_name, COUNT(*) as cnt
    FROM fct_revenue
    WHERE transaction_category = %s
    AND DATE(created_at) >= %s AND DATE(created_at) <= %s
    GROUP BY location_name
    ORDER BY cnt DESC
    """,
    ["Retail Wash", "2026-02-01", "2026-02-28"],
)

Warning

Always use parameterized queries (%s placeholders) for user-supplied values. Never use f-strings or string concatenation to build SQL — this exposes you to SQL injection.

docs.title: Available Tables
docs.text: Key tables in the WASHU_RINSED_SHARE.MB schema:

docs.title: Fact Tables
docs.text: Table Description Key Columns FCT_REVENUE All revenue transactions created_at (timestamp), location_name, transaction_category, amount, item_name FCT_WASHES Wash records created_at (timestamp), location_name, transaction_category (base wash, redemption, free wash) FCT_MEMBERSHIPS Membership billing created_at (timestamp), location_name, transaction_category, revenue, plan_name, rinsed_membership_id FCT_REDEMPTIONS Member wash redemptions created_at (timestamp), location_name, item_name MEMBER_HISTORY Member lifecycle & churn rinsed_membership_id, created_date, churn_type, churn_date, location_name, plan_name

docs.title: Aggregated Tables
docs.text:
Table Description Key Columns CONVERSION_DAILY Daily conversion metrics created_date (date), location_name, total_washes, eligible_washes, sales, conversion_rate CONVERSION_HOURLY Hourly conversion metrics Same as daily + created_hour (0-23) CONVERSION_SHIFT Shift-level (AM/PM) metrics Same as daily + shift ('AM' or 'PM') ACTIVE_MEMBERS_MONTHLY Monthly active member counts month, location_id, active_event_type, members, definition

Timestamp vs. date columns

FCT_REVENUE, FCT_WASHES, FCT_MEMBERSHIPS, and FCT_REDEMPTIONS use created_at as a timestamp (e.g., 2026-03-18 15:49:03). Use DATE(created_at) when filtering by day:

# CORRECT: cast to date for day-level filtering
df = client.query("SELECT * FROM fct_revenue WHERE DATE(created_at) = '2026-03-18'")

# WRONG: this only matches records at exactly midnight
df = client.query("SELECT * FROM fct_revenue WHERE created_at = '2026-03-18'")

CONVERSION_DAILY uses created_date as a date — no casting needed.

docs.title: List All Tables
docs.text:
df = client.query("SHOW TABLES")
for _, row in df.iterrows():
    print(row["name"])

docs.title: Inspect Table Columns
docs.text:
df = client.query("SHOW COLUMNS IN TABLE fct_revenue")
for _, row in df.iterrows():
    print(f"{row['column_name']}: {row['data_type']}")

docs.title: Explore Distinct Values
docs.text:
# What transaction categories exist in FCT_REVENUE?
df = client.query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT transaction_category, COUNT(*) as cnt
    FROM fct_revenue
    GROUP BY transaction_category
    ORDER BY cnt DESC
""")
print(df.to_string())
# Retail Wash                 97865
# Renewed Membership          41598
# Free Wash                    7927
# New Membership & Redemption  7538
# ...

docs.title: Sample Data
docs.text:
df = client.query("SELECT * FROM fct_revenue LIMIT 5")
print(df.columns.tolist())
print(df.to_string())

docs.title: Custom Date Aggregation
docs.text:
The built-in KPI methods aggregate to totals. For daily or weekly granularity, use raw queries:

# Daily wash counts for a month
df = client.query("""
    SELECT created_date, location_name, total_washes, sales, conversion_rate
    FROM conversion_daily
    WHERE created_date >= '2026-02-01' AND created_date <= '2026-02-28'
    AND location_name = 'Burbank'
    ORDER BY created_date
""")
print(df.to_string())

docs.title: Hourly Patterns
docs.text:
# Hourly conversion rates for a specific day
df = client.query("""
    SELECT created_hour, SUM(total_washes) as washes, SUM(sales) as sales
    FROM conversion_hourly
    WHERE created_date = '2026-03-18'
    AND location_name = 'Burbank'
    GROUP BY created_hour
    ORDER BY created_hour
""")

docs.title: Shift-Level Analysis
docs.text:
# AM vs PM conversion rates
df = client.query("""
    SELECT shift,
           SUM(sales) as total_sales,
           SUM(eligible_washes) as total_eligible,
           SUM(sales)::float / NULLIF(SUM(eligible_washes), 0) as rate
    FROM conversion_shift
    WHERE created_date >= '2026-02-01' AND created_date <= '2026-02-28'
    GROUP BY shift
""")

docs.title: Membership Plan Breakdown
docs.text:
# Revenue by membership plan
df = client.query("""
    SELECT plan_name,
           transaction_category,
           COUNT(*) as transactions,
           SUM(revenue) as total_revenue
    FROM fct_memberships
    WHERE DATE(created_at) >= '2026-02-01' AND DATE(created_at) <= '2026-02-28'
    AND location_name = 'Burbank'
    GROUP BY plan_name, transaction_category
    ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
""")

docs.title: Item-Level Wash Analysis
docs.text:
# Which wash packages sell the most?
df = client.query("""
    SELECT item_name, COUNT(*) as cnt, SUM(amount) as revenue
    FROM fct_revenue
    WHERE transaction_category = 'Retail Wash'
    AND DATE(created_at) >= '2026-02-01' AND DATE(created_at) <= '2026-02-28'
    GROUP BY item_name
    ORDER BY cnt DESC
""")

docs.title: Export to CSV
docs.text:
with RinsedClient() as client:
    df = client.query("""
        SELECT created_date, location_name, total_washes, sales, eligible_washes
        FROM conversion_daily
        WHERE created_date >= '2026-01-01' AND created_date <= '2026-02-28'
        ORDER BY created_date, location_name
    """)
    df.to_csv("conversion_data.csv", index=False)
    print(f"Exported {len(df)} rows")

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Bad SQL raises QueryError with the first 100 characters of the query for debugging:

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient, QueryError

with RinsedClient() as client:
    try:
        df = client.query("SELECT * FROM nonexistent_table")
    except QueryError as e:
        print(f"Query failed: {e}")

Read-only access

The Snowflake role used by this client should only have SELECT permissions. Never execute INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or DDL statements through this client.

docs.title: Stats & KPIs
docs.text:
All KPI methods are accessed via client.stats and share the same signature pattern:

client.stats.<method>(start, end, locations=None)

docs.title: Total Car Count
docs.text:
Returns the total number of washes across all types — retail, member redemptions, and free/comped washes. Sourced from the pre-aggregated CONVERSION_DAILY table.

result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Total washes: {result.total:,}")  # 288,389

Use case — daily volume check:

result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-03-18", "2026-03-18")
for loc in result.by_location:
    print(f"{loc.location_name}: {loc.value:,.0f}")
# Burbank: 1,471
# Des Plaines: 1,142
# Carol Stream: 1,014
# ...

docs.title: Retail Car Count
docs.text:
Counts pure retail (non-member) wash transactions from FCT_REVENUE where transaction_category = 'Retail Wash'.

result = client.stats.retail_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Retail washes: {result.total:,}")  # 97,865

What counts as retail?

Only transactions categorized as Retail Wash in FCT_REVENUE. This excludes:

  • NM&R (New Membership & Redemption) — customer bought a membership and got a wash in the same transaction. This is a membership wash.
  • RM&R (Renewed Membership & Redemption) — customer renewed and got a wash. Also a membership wash.
  • Free Wash — comped or $0 transactions.

If you need all non-member tunnel washes including NM&R/RM&R combos, use total_car_count minus member_car_count.

docs.title: Member Car Count
docs.text:
Counts membership redemptions — washes where a member scanned their unlimited pass. Sourced from FCT_WASHES where transaction_category = 'redemption'.

result = client.stats.member_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Member washes: {result.total:,}")  # 174,261

docs.title: Deriving Free Wash Count
docs.text:
There is no dedicated free_car_count() method. Derive it from the other counts:

total = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
retail = client.stats.retail_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
member = client.stats.member_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

# Free washes = total - retail - member - NM&R/RM&R combos
# For an approximation:
free_approx = total.total - retail.total - member.total

Note

This approximation includes NM&R/RM&R combo washes in the "free" bucket. These combo washes are counted in total_car_count but not in retail_car_count or member_car_count. For exact free wash counts, use client.query() against FCT_WASHES directly.

docs.title: Use Case — Member Mix Ratio
docs.text:
Calculate what percentage of washes are from members:

total = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
member = client.stats.member_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

member_pct = member.total / total.total * 100
print(f"Member mix: {member_pct:.1f}%")  # ~60.4%

docs.title: Retail Revenue
docs.text:
Revenue from retail (non-member) wash transactions only. NM&R and RM&R combo revenue is excluded — those dollars are membership revenue.

result = client.stats.retail_revenue("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Revenue: ${result.total:,.2f}")              # $1,410,932.00
print(f"Transactions: {result.transaction_count:,}")  # 97,865

The by_location breakdown shows revenue per site:

for loc in result.by_location:
    print(f"{loc.location_name}: ${loc.value:,.2f}")
# Burbank: $98,234.50
# Des Plaines: $87,654.30
# ...

docs.title: Membership Revenue
docs.text:
Revenue from membership billing — both new sales and renewals — with a breakdown:

result = client.stats.membership_revenue("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Total membership revenue: ${result.total:,.2f}")       # $1,233,481.74
print(f"  New member revenue:     ${result.new_revenue:,.2f}")  # $134,383.54
print(f"  Renewal revenue:        ${result.renewal_revenue:,.2f}")  # $1,099,098.20

Revenue sources

  • New revenue: new membership + rejoin membership from FCT_MEMBERSHIPS
  • Renewal revenue: renewed membership from FCT_MEMBERSHIPS
  • Total: sum of both

docs.title: Use Case — Total Revenue
docs.text:
Combine retail and membership revenue for total site revenue:

retail = client.stats.retail_revenue("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
membership = client.stats.membership_revenue("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

total_revenue = retail.total + membership.total
print(f"Total revenue: ${total_revenue:,.2f}")
print(f"  Retail: ${retail.total:,.2f} ({retail.total/total_revenue:.1%})")
print(f"  Membership: ${membership.total:,.2f} ({membership.total/total_revenue:.1%})")

docs.title: Average Wash Price (AWP)
docs.text:
The retail ticket average: retail_revenue / retail_car_count. This tells you what the average non-member customer pays per wash.

result = client.stats.average_wash_price("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"AWP: ${result.awp:.2f}")                    # $14.42
print(f"Retail revenue: ${result.retail_revenue:,.2f}")  # $1,410,932.00
print(f"Retail cars: {result.retail_car_count:,}")    # 97,865

Zero retail washes

If there are zero retail washes in the period (e.g., a location with only members), AWP returns 0.0 rather than raising a division error.

docs.title: Use Case — AWP by Location
docs.text:
AWP is only available as an aggregate. To get per-location AWP, use the component methods:

rev = client.stats.retail_revenue("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
cars = client.stats.retail_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

rev_map = {loc.location_name: loc.value for loc in rev.by_location}
cars_map = {loc.location_name: loc.value for loc in cars.by_location}

for name in sorted(rev_map):
    r = rev_map[name]
    c = cars_map.get(name, 0)
    awp = r / c if c > 0 else 0
    print(f"{name}: ${awp:.2f} ({c:,.0f} cars, ${r:,.2f} revenue)")

docs.title: New Membership Sales
docs.text:
Count of new and rejoin membership transactions from FCT_MEMBERSHIPS. Includes both brand-new members and members who previously cancelled and came back.

result = client.stats.new_membership_sales("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"New members: {result.total:,}")              # 8,479
print(f"Revenue: ${result.total_revenue:,.2f}")       # $134,383.54

Per-location breakdown:

for loc in result.by_location:
    print(f"{loc.location_name}: {loc.value:,.0f} new members")

docs.title: Use Case — Revenue Per New Member
docs.text:
result = client.stats.new_membership_sales("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
rev_per_member = result.total_revenue / result.total if result.total > 0 else 0
print(f"Average first-month revenue per new member: ${rev_per_member:.2f}")

docs.title: Conversion Rate
docs.text:
The percentage of eligible washes that convert into membership sales. Sourced from the pre-aggregated CONVERSION_DAILY table.

Formula: conversion_rate = sales / eligible_washes

result = client.stats.conversion_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Rate: {result.rate:.1%}")            # 8.0%
print(f"Sales: {result.sales:,}")             # 8,479
print(f"Eligible: {result.eligible_washes:,}")  # 106,008

docs.title: Per-Location Conversion Rates
docs.text:
The by_location field contains each location's individual conversion rate (not a count):

result = client.stats.conversion_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

# Sort by conversion rate, highest first
ranked = sorted(result.by_location, key=lambda x: x.value, reverse=True)
for i, loc in enumerate(ranked, 1):
    print(f"{i}. {loc.location_name}: {loc.value:.1%}")

What are eligible washes?

Eligible washes are non-member, non-free washes that had the opportunity to convert. This is a pre-calculated field in Rinsed's CONVERSION_DAILY table. It closely matches (but is not identical to) the retail car count from FCT_REVENUE.

docs.title: Edge Case — Zero Eligible Washes
docs.text:
Locations or periods with no eligible washes return a rate of 0.0:

# Querying a date when a location was closed
result = client.stats.conversion_rate("2026-12-25", "2026-12-25", locations="Fairview")
print(result.rate)  # 0.0

docs.title: Bundled Report
docs.text:
Get all KPIs (except churn) in a single call:

report = client.stats.report("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

print(f"Total cars:        {report.total_car_count.total:,}")
print(f"Retail cars:       {report.retail_car_count.total:,}")
print(f"Member cars:       {report.member_car_count.total:,}")
print(f"Retail revenue:    ${report.retail_revenue.total:,.2f}")
print(f"Membership revenue:${report.membership_revenue.total:,.2f}")
print(f"AWP:               ${report.average_wash_price.awp:.2f}")
print(f"New sales:         {report.new_membership_sales.total:,}")
print(f"Conversion:        {report.conversion.rate:.1%}")

Performance

report() executes 8+ Snowflake queries internally (one per KPI, plus AWP calls retail_revenue and retail_car_count). For a single month, this typically takes 2-5 seconds depending on network latency.

docs.title: Use Case — Monthly Report Export
docs.text:
import json

with RinsedClient() as client:
    report = client.stats.report("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

    # Export to JSON
    with open("feb_2026_kpis.json", "w") as f:
        f.write(report.model_dump_json(indent=2))

docs.title: Use Case — Month-Over-Month Comparison
docs.text:
with RinsedClient() as client:
    jan = client.stats.report("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    feb = client.stats.report("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

    car_change = (feb.total_car_count.total - jan.total_car_count.total) / jan.total_car_count.total
    rev_change = (feb.retail_revenue.total - jan.retail_revenue.total) / jan.retail_revenue.total
    conv_change = feb.conversion.rate - jan.conversion.rate

    print(f"Car count:  {car_change:+.1%}")
    print(f"Revenue:    {rev_change:+.1%}")
    print(f"Conversion: {conv_change:+.1%} pts")

docs.title: Use Case — Location Scoreboard
docs.text:
with RinsedClient() as client:
    report = client.stats.report("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

    # Build per-location scoreboard from report components
    locations = set()
    for kpi in [report.total_car_count, report.retail_revenue, report.conversion]:
        for loc in kpi.by_location:
            locations.add(loc.location_name)

    cars_map = {l.location_name: l.value for l in report.total_car_count.by_location}
    rev_map = {l.location_name: l.value for l in report.retail_revenue.by_location}
    conv_map = {l.location_name: l.value for l in report.conversion.by_location}

    print(f"{'Location':<16} {'Cars':>8} {'Revenue':>12} {'Conv':>8}")
    print("-" * 46)
    for loc in sorted(locations):
        cars = cars_map.get(loc, 0)
        rev = rev_map.get(loc, 0)
        conv = conv_map.get(loc, 0)
        print(f"{loc:<16} {cars:>8,.0f} ${rev:>10,.2f} {conv:>7.1%}")

docs.title: Combo Transactions (NM&R / RM&R)
docs.text:
When a customer buys or renews a membership and gets a wash in the same transaction:

  • The wash counts as a member wash (in member_car_count via FCT_WASHES)
  • The revenue goes to membership revenue (in membership_revenue via FCT_MEMBERSHIPS)
  • It does not appear in retail_car_count or retail_revenue

docs.title: Family Plans
docs.text: Locations with significant family plan populations (notably Dickson and Fairview) have many $0 renewal records. When a family plan renews, the primary member is billed the full amount and each additional family member gets a $0 renewal row. All individual family members are counted in membership counts.

docs.title: DRB to Sonny's POS Migration
docs.text:
Illinois locations transitioned from DRB to Sonny's POS in two phases:

  • Phase 1 (Dec 2024 / Jan 2025): First group of IL sites
  • Phase 2 (Apr / May 2025): Plainfield, Burbank, Berwyn, Joliet, Evergreen Park

During migration months, there may be slight discrepancies due to overlapping location IDs. Data outside migration months is unaffected.

docs.title: Snowflake Data Freshness
docs.text: Snowflake data is not real-time. There is typically a 24-48 hour lag before data appears. For real-time data, use the Sonny's Data API Client.
